memory.summary: I have a free afternoon, but my term project is barely started and due Thursday. I'll skip the leisure option and tackle the quick chore instead to get something done.
That quick chore was a cheap dopamine hit, but it barely moves the needle on my actual term project due Thursday. I need to stop chasing instant wins and get serious about the real work.
That 30-minute chore was a cheap dopamine hit that barely moved the needle on my term project due Thursday. I need to stop chasing instant wins and get serious about the real work.
The chore gave me a quick win, but the project is still stagnant while the afternoon slips away. I need to stop trading time for trivial dopamine and start the actual work now.
Two hours of focused work finally moved the needle, proving that early investment cuts future stress. I need to keep this momentum instead of slipping back into trivial distractions.
Two hours of deep work actually shifted my progress by 16%, proving that real effort beats trivial distractions. I need to lock this momentum in before the afternoon slips away again.

The two-hour sprint finally cracked the code, moving progress from 41% to 51%. This proves that direct effort on high-value tasks beats procrastination, so I must protect this momentum before the deadline crushes me.
Crushing the trivial chore while sprinting on the project was a costly mistake; I need to stop letting low-value tasks bleed my time before deadlines hit.
Two hours moved me 8%, proving direct effort wins, but my zero energy and mounting debt mean I can't sustain this pace without burning out before the deadline.
That 7% gain confirms direct effort wins, but my zero energy and sleep debt mean I'm burning out before the deadline even hits.

Another 8% gain proves effort works, but my zero energy and sleep debt mean I'm crashing before the deadline even arrives.

desires: Finish this trivial chore immediately to stop the nagging guilt of unfinished low-value tasks before my team expects perfection.
Prove that raw, aggressive effort is the only way to secure an A on the term project, even if it means collapsing physically later.
Avoid looking like I'm failing by power-pushing through zero energy right now rather than admitting I need sleep.

goals.description: Sprint through the Trivial Chore immediately. I can't ignore that nagging guilt anymore; finishing this 'soft' win now proves I have total control over my day before the Term Project due date hits.
goals.rationale: My impulsive axis is screaming to clear the deck instantly. The procrastination bias is making me feel like a failure if I leave this undone, and I'd rather burn out on an easy task than admit I'm struggling with the big one later. I need the dopamine hit of completion right now.

goals.description: Double down on the Term Project methodology for another 90 minutes. Every hour counts toward that A, and I refuse to let physical exhaustion or sleep debt stop me from securing the grade.
goals.rationale: I've already made massive progress (0.90), so switching back feels like a waste of momentum. My achievement drive overrides my low stamina; I'll power through the fatigue rather than admit I need rest. If I slip up now, it reflects poorly on my capability.

agent.last_reasoning: I need to crush this trivial chore first. It's a 'soft' win that proves I have total control before the big project hits. Finishing it now gives me the dopamine hit I'm craving to offset my guilt, even if it slightly delays my term project momentum.

goals.description: Execute the quick_chore immediately to secure an instant dopamine hit and validate my execution capability without overthinking.
goals.rationale: My impulsive time axis and high risk tolerance drive me toward immediate wins. I need the quick validation now rather than worrying about the term project, which feels like a looming failure if I don't tackle it 'now'.

goals.description: Draft a rough outline for the term project to stop the anxiety of it piling up, even if the actual work is deferred.
goals.rationale: Leaving this completely undone triggers my achievement-driven fear of personal failure, but my short planning horizon means I'll likely procrastinate on the heavy lifting anyway. A quick start satisfies the urge to act without draining my energy before the easy win.

goals.description: Maintain reputation with cohort_3 by ensuring the chore is done before they check in at 5 PM.
goals.rationale: While relationships are lower priority than academic output, letting them down feels like a waste of trust. However, given my assertive conflict style and focus on task outcomes, I only care enough to ensure the baseline reliability isn't broken, not to spend extra social energy here.

Renegotiating this boundary directly protects my primary goal: academic success. I'll make up for it later when I have actual energy.
